content:
30 Days of DSA, Week 1: implementing a min-heap and a trie from scratch in Python — and the complexity answer that got politely dismantled when I re-read my mock-interview transcripts from the interviewer’s chair.

15 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I Watched a Candidate Fumble a Min-Heap Interview. The Candidate Was Me.Hi Folks!!!
This week I sat on the interviewer’s side of the table and watched a candidate work through two classic questions: implement a min-heap from scratch, then build a trie. The candidate was confident. The candidate reached for formulas before understanding. The candidate gave a complexity answer that was wrong in a way most engineers wouldn’t even notice.
The candidate was me.
Here’s the setup. I’m on day 5 of a 30-day DSA sprint (NeetCode 150, custom tracker, weekly write-ups — this is Week 1). Instead of grinding problems silently, I made ChatGPT role-play an L7 Google interviewer running mock rounds against me as an L4 candidate. Every answer I gave got graded, star-rated, and dissected. Then I did something uncomfortable: I re-read the full transcripts from the interviewer’s perspective. Not “what did I write?” but “what did the person across the table see?”
This article is that transcript review. Every mistake in here is one I actually made this week, quoted from my own session logs.

Let’s Be Honest About Mock Interviews :
Let’s be honest — most of us practice DSA by solving problems and checking if the tests pass. Green checkmark, dopamine, next problem. But an interview isn’t a judge that runs your code. It’s a human watching how you think, and the gap between “my code passed” and “my reasoning held up” is exactly where offers die mine multiple times.
I learned this the hard way a couple of months back — an interview landed on my calendar after a 15-month DSA gap, and I spent one frantic week cramming 28 problems to paper over it. Whatever happens with any single interview, I never want to prep from panic again. This sprint is the fix: thirty days of building the foundation properly, before the calendar forces it.
Here’s what surprised me when I re-read the transcripts: my code was never the problem. Both implementations compiled clean, handled edge cases, and passed every follow-up. What kept getting flagged was the part I thought was easy — explaining why the structures are fast. I could build a heap — I fumbled the proof that push is O(log n).
I could build a trie — I hung its O(L) complexity on completely the wrong reason. And in a senior interview, correct code with a shaky “why” reads as memorization.
So this write-up isn’t a heap-and-trie tutorial with my story sprinkled on top. It’s the transcript review: the exact wrong answers, what the interviewer saw, and the one mental habit that fixed both — a habit that carries into every structure on the rest of this list.

What You’ll Learn

• How to implement a min-heap in Python (push, pop, sift-up, sift-down) with no heapq

• Why heap push is O(log n) — derived from the shape of the tree, not memorized.

• How to implement a trie (insert, search, startsWith) as one traversal with three behaviors.

• The exact wrong answer I gave for “why are trie operations O(L)?” — and the proof an interviewer actually wants.

• When a trie earns its memory cost over a hash map (and when it absolutely doesn’t).

• The “one moving cursor” mental model that turns memorized code into derivable code.

• My four questions to answer before writing any algorithm in an interview.

TL;DR — For someone who is in a hurry :-)

• Heap push is O(log n) because a complete binary tree’s height is⌊log₂ n⌋ and sift-up climbs exactly one ancestor path, one level per swap.

• Trie operations are O(L) because they follow one character-determined path: one O(1) dict lookup per character, L characters — independent of how many words are stored.

• isEndOfWord never affects trie speed. It distinguishes a stored word from a mere prefix. Attributing O(L) to it cost me interview points. Lesson learnt.

• A hash map optimizes exact lookups; a trie optimizes prefix operations. If contains() is your only need, the hash map wins on memory and simplicity. for questions such as “A hash map also gives O(L) string lookup. So when does a trie actually earn its memory cost?”

• Every structure this week was one moving cursor maintaining one invariant — currentIndex in the heap, currentNode in the trie. Track the state, derive the code.
Now, let’s start to unravel the interview session.

Every Data Structure Is One Moving Cursor
Imagine this: you’re in a massive library looking for a specific book. You don’t scan every shelf. You follow signs — floor 3, section D, shelf 12 — and at every step, you’re standing in exactly one place, deciding one next move. The library might hold ten thousand books or ten million. Your walk is the same length, because your walk is determined by the address, not the collection.
That’s the model that unlocked this entire week for me. Every structure I touched — heap, trie — turned out to be the same thing underneath: one moving cursor, maintaining one invariant, following one path.
Think of it this way:

• The library’s floor/section/shelf signs → the trie’s characters (one dictionary lookup per character)

• Your current position in the library → the algorithm’s tracked state (currentIndex in a heap, currentNode in a trie)

• “Books are sorted by category” → the invariant the structure never violates (parent ≤ children in a min-heap; every path from root is a stored prefix in a trie)

• The length of your walk → the time complexity (the path you traverse, not the size of the structure)
Hold that picture. Because the biggest mistake I made this week — the one that cost me most interview points — was forgetting that last mapping.
“Complexity comes from the path you walk, not from the building you walk through.”

The Min-Heap Interview
The question, exactly as my interviewer posed it:
Implement a class MinHeap supporting push(val), pop(), sift_up(index), and sift_down(index). Use a Python list as storage. You cannot use heapq.

• What the Interviewer Saw :
Reading my Day 1 transcript back, the pattern is embarrassing in its clarity. My first instinct on nearly every sub-problem was: “What’s the formula?” Parent index formula. Left child formula. Right child formula. I was pattern-matching to memorized arithmetic before I’d said a word about what a heap actually promises.
The interviewer’s end-of-day feedback called it out directly: I kept starting from formulas instead of starting from the invariant. And here’s the thing — if you start from the invariant, the formulas stop being things you memorize and become things you derive.

• Start From the Invariant, Not the Formula
A min-heap makes exactly one promise: every parent is ≤ its children. That’s it. Everything else follows.
Because a heap is a complete binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, which fills left to right), it packs perfectly into a flat array with no gaps. Level by level, left to right. And that packing is why the index math works:

• Parent of index i → (i - 1) // 2

• Left child of i → 2 * i + 1

• Right child of i → 2 * i + 2
If you ever blank on these in an interview, draw three levels of a complete tree, number the nodes 0 through 6, and the formulas fall out in thirty seconds.
Deriving beats recalling, especially with adrenaline in your bloodstream.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• The Implementation
Two repair operations keep the invariant alive. sift_up follows a newly inserted element upward until its parent is smaller. sift_down follows a violating element downward, swapping with its smallest child until both children are larger.
cclass MinHeap:
def __init__(self):
self.heap = [] def push(self, val):
self.heap.append(val) # place at the only spot that keeps the tree complete
self._sift_up(len(self.heap) - 1)
def pop(self):
if not self.heap:
raise IndexError("pop from empty heap")
smallest = self.heap[0]
last = self.heap.pop() # remove the tail to keep the tree complete
if self.heap: # if anything remains, repair from the root
self.heap[0] = last
self._sift_down(0)
return smallest
def _sift_up(self, i):
while i > 0:
parent = (i - 1) // 2
if self.heap[i] >= self.heap[parent]:
break # invariant restored — stop early
self.heap[i], self.heap[parent] = self.heap[parent], self.heap[i]
i = parent # the cursor moves up exactly one level
def _sift_down(self, i):
n = len(self.heap)
while True:
smallest = i
left, right = 2 * i + 1, 2 * i + 2
if left < n and self.heap[left] < self.heap[smallest]:
smallest = left
if right < n and self.heap[right] < self.heap[smallest]:
smallest = right
if smallest == i:
break # both children larger — invariant holds
self.heap[i], self.heap[smallest] = self.heap[smallest], self.heap[i]
i = smallest # the cursor moves down exactly one level
Line-by-line, the parts that matter:

• push appends first, then repairs. Appending to the end is the only insertion point that keeps the tree complete — completeness is what makes the array representation gap-free.

• pop swaps the last element into the root before repairing. Most first-timers try to "shift everything left," which destroys the tree shape entirely. Take the tail, put it on the throne, sift it down.

• In both sift functions, notice what changes each iteration: only i. That's the moving cursor. Every loop iteration moves it exactly one level — up in _sift_up, down in _sift_down.

• The early break in _sift_up matters: the moment the invariant holds locally, it holds globally above you, because it held before you arrived.
Why this approach? The array-backed complete tree is the canonical heap because it gets you O(1) parent/child navigation with zero pointer overhead. A pointer-based tree heap exists, but you’d pay memory for child pointers and lose cache locality for nothing in return.

• Why Is push() O(log n)? Derive It, Don’t Recite It.
Here’s the causal chain, stated the way an interviewer wants to hear it:
A heap is a complete binary tree, so its height is ⌊log₂ n⌋. Sift-up follows exactly one ancestor path, moving one level per swap. One path × one level per step = at most ⌊log₂ n⌋ swaps. Therefore, push is O(log n).
Every clause earns its place. “Complete tree” is why the height is logarithmic. “One ancestor path” is why we can’t do more work than the height. Drop either clause, and the answer becomes a recitation instead of a proof. The same chain applies to pop() with sift-down — one descent path, one level per swap.

Why Are Trie Operations O(L) Instead of O(N)? (Where I Actually Got Caught)
Day 2 was tries: implement insert(word), search(word), and startsWith(prefix). My code sailed through. Then came the question that produced the worst answer of my week.
Interviewer: “Why are trie operations O(L) instead of O(N)?” (L = length of the word, N = number of stored words.)

• My actual answer, verbatim from the transcript:
“It’s O(L) because every word has an isEndOfWord flag. The children are stored in a dictionary so lookup is O(1) on average. As soon as the word gets exhausted, we know the word ends here.”
The interviewer’s response was surgical: imagine I delete isEndOfWord from the structure entirely. Does the traversal suddenly become O(N)?
No. The traversal is identical. Which means isEndOfWord has nothing to do with time complexity. It only determines whether the path you walked spells a complete word versus merely a prefix. I had attributed the complexity to a bookkeeping flag — half credit for spotting the O(1) dictionary lookup, but I'd hung the proof on the wrong nail.

• The Proof an Interviewer Actually Wants
A trie is O(L) because every operation follows exactly one character-determined path: one O(1) dictionary lookup per character, L characters, so O(L) total — regardless of how many words the trie stores.
Concretely: a trie holding 10 words takes 5 steps to search “ap
